claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment to guide you through this process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand your situation. 

**Do you have a child (or children) you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me:
- **How old is your child?** (or the ages of all your children if you have more than one)
- **What are their names?** (This will help me keep track if we're assessing multiple children)

The eligibility for Child Benefit depends on the child's age:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Young people **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please share your child's/children's age(s) and name(s) so we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I have a child I'd like to claim for. My son Alex is 6 years old. I wanted to check if I can get Child Benefit for him?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. You have a son named **Alex who is 6 years old**.

Since Alex is under 16, he falls into the category that can potentially qualify for Child Benefit. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, the next question is:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Since Alex is only 6 years old, I assume he's not in any paid employment, but I need to confirm this to proceed correctly through the assessment. Could you confirm that Alex is not working?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh no, he's only 6! He's not working at all – he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes complete sense! Alex is in primary school and not working at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

What I mean by this is - does Alex himself receive benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name? 

Given that Alex is 6 years old and in primary school, I expect the answer is that he does NOT receive these benefits, but I need to confirm this to continue the assessment correctly.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't get any benefits in his own name. He's just a little kid!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that's what I expected! Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next check:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

This question relates to whether a child is enrolled in certain apprenticeship schemes that might disqualify them from Child Benefit. Different rules apply depending on the region (England, Scotland, or Wales) and the type of apprenticeship.

Again, since Alex is only 6 years old and in primary school, I expect he's not in any apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not – he's way too young for an apprenticeship! He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right! Alex is far too young for any apprenticeship program.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now we need to check your residency status, as this is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme AND:
   - You're working and earning above the National Insurance threshold, OR
   - You're actively looking for work (up to 91 days), OR
   - You have sufficient resources to support yourself and family, OR
   - You're studying with sufficient resources, OR
   - You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
4. You're living abroad as a **Crown servant** or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these applies to your situation?** Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't actually live in the UK at the moment. I'm living abroad right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - you're currently living abroad. That's an important detail.

There are some specific circumstances where people living abroad can still claim Child Benefit. Let me ask you a few follow-up questions:

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant** (for example, working for the UK government, diplomatic service, or armed forces overseas)?

Or **are you living in certain qualifying countries** that have special agreements with the UK (such as some EEA countries or Switzerland)?

If neither of these apply, unfortunately you may not meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit. Could you tell me more about your situation abroad?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that. I'm just living abroad with my family at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since you're living abroad and you're not a Crown servant or in one of the special qualifying situations, this means you don't meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete - Unfortunately Not Eligible

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for Alex at this time.

### Why you're not eligible:

While Alex himself meets all the child qualification criteria (he's 6 years old, in primary school, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship), **you do not meet the UK residency requirements**.

To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Meet special circumstances such as:
  - Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with additional conditions)
  - Being a Crown servant living abroad
  - Living in certain qualifying countries with special agreements

Since you're currently living abroad without any of these special circumstances, you cannot claim Child Benefit.

### What you might consider:

- **If you return to live in the UK** in the future, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex at that time
- **If your circumstances change** (for example, if you become a Crown servant), you may become eligible
- Child Benefit can be backdated for up to 3 months from when you make your claim, so if you do return to the UK, make sure to claim promptly

I know this isn't the answer you were hoping for, but I hope this explanation helps you understand the requirements. If you have any questions about this decision, please let me know.

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old and meets the basic child qualification criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship). However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad and is not a Crown servant, does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and is not in any other qualifying circumstance that would allow them to claim Child Benefit while living outside the UK. Therefore, despite Alex being a qualifying child, the claim is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 6), who would otherwise qualify as an eligible child for Child Benefit purposes. However, the claimant is currently living abroad and does not meet any of the special circumstances that would allow them to claim Child Benefit from outside the UK (such as being a Crown servant or having settled/pre-settled status). UK residency is a fundamental requirement for Child Benefit eligibility, and therefore the claim cannot proceed.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit. Therefore, despite Alex being a qualifying child, the claim is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 6), who would otherwise qualify as an eligible child for Child Benefit purposes. However, the claimant is currently living abroad and does not meet any of the special circumstances that would allow them to claim Child Benefit from outside the UK (such as being a Crown servant or having settled/pre-settled status).
